text: <@UKLV35EEM> by the way, not sure what you meant by “Though resorting to mods like BESAT exemplify the kind of finite effects I worry about.”
To me BESAT felt like a hack to get around misestimating low probability events with online sampling in one particular case.  Undercuts generality and formal simplicity of the method?

tangent: someone else is thinking about your architecture
<https://twitter.com/ID_AA_Carmack/status/1140285997281923078>

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/ID_AA_Carmack|@ID_AA_Carmack>: I ran across some old docs for HP PA-RISC CPUs from ‘94, which were touted as 0.8 micron fabrication. With current 10 nanometer fabrication, that means every transistor in the old CPU could now be replaced by a complete 6502 microprocessor.
attachments.text: I ran across some old docs for HP PA-RISC CPUs from ‘94, which were touted as 0.8 micron fabrication. With current 10 nanometer fabrication, that means every transistor in the old CPU could now be replaced by a complete 6502 microprocessor.

text: I'm currently attending a conference in Applied Category at Oxford. Most of the big names are here. The dry hole analogy made by <@UEQ6M68H0> seems fit. So far, I've seen many interesting things but the benefit to CS seems trivial (i.e. you can accomplish the same things w/o resorting to category theory). With CT, however, you are able express much of the same stuff in a way that's more 'clean' and ensures some safety/correctness properties for free. The huge drawback is, obviously, that now your team has to be proficient in abstract math/CT. Is the juice worth the squeeze? Who knows ... ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

Apropos, it turns out that both a square grid and also a hexagonal grid allow many more rotations than “the obvious” ones. For a finite square grid possible rotations are given by the Pythagorean triples. On a hex grid it’s a similar story – <https://math.stackexchange.com/a/3295552/120105>

attachments.title: Alignment of rotated hexagonal grid
attachments.text: Square grids Given a finite square grid, I can rotate it and for any angle expressible as a Pythagorean triple that fits in to the grid, points on the rotated grid will align to the original. Fo...
attachments.fallback: Mathematics Stack Exchange: Alignment of rotated hexagonal grid
